skills.name: abyss-safe-infra-change
skills.description: Apply the aoa-safe-infra-change workflow inside an abyss-* repository using repo-relative operational surfaces, explicit local authority notes, rollback posture, and local validation commands. Use when the base infra-change workflow is correct but a thin project overlay is needed for one abyss repo. Do not use when the base skill is sufficient without local adaptation or when the task is really about producing a shareable artifact.
skills.compatibility: Designed for Codex or similar coding agents with repository file access and an interactive shell. Network access is optional and only needed when repository validation or referenced workflows require it.

section_summaries.Intent: Use this skill to adapt `aoa-safe-infra-change` to an `abyss-*` repository when the base operational workflow is correct but the local repo still needs repo-relative commands, authority notes, and risk framing.
section_summaries.Trigger boundary: the base `aoa-safe-infra-change` workflow is already correct, but an `abyss-*` repo needs repo-relative operational surfaces, commands, or approval notes; the task is a bounded infrastructure, service, configuration, or operational change inside one local repo family
section_summaries.Inputs: target operational change and touched local surface; repo-relative operational files or commands
section_summaries.Outputs: bounded local infra-change plan; repo-relative command or path sketch
section_summaries.Procedure: start from `aoa-safe-infra-change` instead of inventing a new project-family workflow; name the repo-relative operational files, commands, and authority posture that matter locally
section_summaries.Contracts: preserve the base skill meaning; keep paths and commands repo-relative
section_summaries.Risks and anti-patterns: hiding downstream authority inside vague local operational notes; turning a thin overlay into project doctrine or a scenario bundle
section_summaries.Verification: confirm the base skill is still the correct workflow; confirm repo-relative paths and commands are named explicitly
section_summaries.Technique traceability: AOA-T-0028 from `8Dionysus/aoa-techniques` at `609693c2782510e0811ba7ecb4904bc06cf40c38` using path `techniques/agent-workflows/confirmation-gated-mutating-action/TECHNIQUE.md` and sections: Intent, When to use, Inputs, Outputs, Core procedure, Contracts, Risks, Validation; AO...
section_summaries.Adaptation points: repo-relative operational surfaces; local authority and approval notes

interface.default_prompt: Use $abyss-safe-infra-change to apply the base infra-change workflow with repo-relative abyss paths, explicit local authority, and local verification notes.

collision_notes: Use a project overlay only when the repository actually matches that overlay family and the repo-relative adaptation matters.
Current live overlay families here are the atm10-* and abyss-* repo-local exemplars.
Use the base AoA skill when the workflow does not need a thin project overlay.

explicit: Use $abyss-safe-infra-change to apply the base infra-change workflow with repo-relative abyss paths, explicit local authority, and local verification notes.
manual_invocation_required: Apply the aoa-safe-infra-change workflow inside an abyss-* repository using repo-relative operational surfaces, explicit local authority notes, rollback posture, and local validation commands. Use when the base infra-change workflow is correct but a thin project overlay is needed for one abyss repo.
Inside this abyss-stack repo, plan a bounded infra change with repo-relative abyss paths, explicit local authority, and rollback thinking.
negative: This repository is not an abyss-* repo and the base infra-change workflow already names the needed operational surface without any project-specific overlay.

skills.name: aoa-adr-write
skills.description: Capture a meaningful architectural or workflow decision as an ADR or decision note, place it in the canonical decision surface, and verify the rationale stays discoverable. Use when structure, boundaries, tooling, or workflow expectations change and future contributors need to know why. Do not use for tiny self-evident edits or when the real problem is source-of-truth ambiguity rather than decision rationale.
skills.compatibility: Designed for Codex or similar coding agents with repository file access and an interactive shell. Network access is optional and only needed when repository validation or referenced workflows require it.

section_summaries.Intent: Use this skill to capture an architectural, structural, or workflow decision in a concise reviewable note, then place and verify that note in the canonical repo surface so the rationale stays findable.
section_summaries.Trigger boundary: a decision changes structure, boundaries, tooling, or workflow expectations; future contributors will need to know why a path was chosen
section_summaries.Inputs: decision to record; context and problem statement
section_summaries.Outputs: concise decision note or ADR draft; statement of rationale
section_summaries.Procedure: state the context and the problem the decision addresses; list the main options if they meaningfully shaped the choice
section_summaries.Contracts: the note should explain why, not just what changed; the decision should be bounded and understandable
section_summaries.Risks and anti-patterns: writing an ADR for a trivial edit with no real decision; using inflated language to mask weak reasoning
section_summaries.Verification: confirm the decision was meaningful enough to record; confirm the rationale is explicit
section_summaries.Technique traceability: AOA-T-0033 from `8Dionysus/aoa-techniques` at `609693c2782510e0811ba7ecb4904bc06cf40c38` using path `techniques/docs/decision-rationale-recording/TECHNIQUE.md` and sections: Intent, When to use, When not to use, Inputs, Outputs, Core procedure, Contracts, Risks, Validation; AO...
section_summaries.Adaptation points: local ADR templates; alternative decision-note homes outside formal `docs/adr/` layouts

interface.default_prompt: Use $aoa-adr-write to record a meaningful architectural or workflow decision, place it in the canonical decision surface, and verify the rationale is easy to find.

collision_notes: Use aoa-adr-write when the task is to capture a concrete architectural or workflow decision.
Use aoa-source-of-truth-check when the task is to identify which documents are authoritative and reduce overlap.

explicit: Use $aoa-adr-write to record a meaningful architectural or workflow decision, place it in the canonical decision surface, and verify the rationale is easy to find.
implicit: Capture a meaningful architectural or workflow decision as an ADR or decision note, place it in the canonical decision surface, and verify the rationale stays discoverable. Use when structure, boundaries, tooling, or workflow expectations change and future contributors need to know why.
negative: The change is tiny and self-evident, with no durable decision or rationale that future contributors would need to find.

skills.disclose_card_markdown:
### aoa-adr-write
- Summary: Record architecture decisions clearly
- Use when: Capture a meaningful architectural or workflow decision as an ADR or decision note, place it in the canonical decision surface, and verify the rationale stays discoverable. Use when structure, boundaries, tooling, or workflow expectations change and future contributors need to know why. Do not use for tiny self-evident edits or when the real problem is source-of-truth ambiguity rather than decision rationale.
- Trust posture: portable-core
- Mutation surface: repo

skills.name: aoa-approval-gate-check
skills.description: Classify whether a requested action is safe to proceed, requires explicit approval, or should not be executed. Use when a task may be destructive, security-sensitive, or operationally sensitive and the authority boundary is unclear. Do not use for clearly low-risk work or when the main need is previewing a mutation rather than deciding whether it may proceed.
skills.compatibility: Designed for Codex or similar coding agents with repository file access and an interactive shell. Network access is optional and only needed when repository validation or referenced workflows require it.

section_summaries.Intent: Use this skill to classify whether a requested action crosses an approval boundary and to return a bounded next step that keeps authority explicit before any execution proceeds.
section_summaries.Trigger boundary: a task may be destructive, operationally sensitive, or security-relevant; the current authority level is unclear
section_summaries.Inputs: requested action; touched surfaces
section_summaries.Outputs: classification of the action: safe to proceed, explicit approval required, or do not execute; note on whether explicit approval is needed
section_summaries.Procedure: identify the requested action and touched surfaces; assess whether the action could be destructive, sensitive, or authority-gated
section_summaries.Contracts: unclear authority should not be silently interpreted as permission; classification should be explicit and reviewable
section_summaries.Risks and anti-patterns: assuming approval because a task sounds routine; collapsing several risk levels into a single vague warning
section_summaries.Verification: confirm the touched surfaces were identified; confirm the approval need was classified explicitly rather than as a vague warning
section_summaries.Technique traceability: AOA-T-0028 from `8Dionysus/aoa-techniques` at `609693c2782510e0811ba7ecb4904bc06cf40c38` using path `techniques/agent-workflows/confirmation-gated-mutating-action/TECHNIQUE.md` and sections: Intent, When to use, Inputs, Outputs, Core procedure, Contracts, Risks, Validation
section_summaries.Adaptation points: local authority models; local risk categories

interface.default_prompt: Use $aoa-approval-gate-check to classify whether the requested action is safe, approval-gated, or out of bounds before any execution proceeds.

collision_notes: aoa-approval-gate-check classifies risk and whether execution is allowed at all.
aoa-dry-run-first insists on preview or simulation before a real mutation.
aoa-safe-infra-change handles bounded infra or config changes with rollback thinking.
aoa-local-stack-bringup is the local multi-service stack start path and is explicit-only.
aoa-sanitized-share is about turning raw technical material into a safe public artifact.

explicit: Use $aoa-approval-gate-check to classify whether the requested action is safe, approval-gated, or out of bounds before any execution proceeds.
manual_invocation_required: Classify whether a requested action is safe to proceed, requires explicit approval, or should not be executed. Use when a task may be destructive, security-sensitive, or operationally sensitive and the authority boundary is unclear.
Before touching production DNS records, classify whether this request is safe, approval-gated, or out of bounds.
negative: The task is clearly low-risk, already bounded, and does not look destructive, security-sensitive, or operationally sensitive.

skills.disclose_card_markdown:

- Use when: Classify whether a requested action is safe to proceed, requires explicit approval, or should not be executed. Do not use for clearly low-risk work or when the main need is previewing a mutation rather than deciding whether it may proceed.

skills.name: aoa-bounded-context-map
skills.description: Clarify system or domain boundaries, name contexts, and surface interfaces so changes stay semantically scoped. Use when naming is overloaded, responsibilities are mixed, or a task needs a cleaner boundary before coding. Do not use for tiny local edits or when the boundary is already clear and the real task is contract validation.
skills.compatibility: Designed for Codex or similar coding agents with repository file access and an interactive shell. Network access is optional and only needed when repository validation or referenced workflows require it.

section_summaries.Intent: Use bounded-context thinking to reduce semantic drift, mixed responsibilities, and unclear interfaces.
section_summaries.Trigger boundary: a project mixes several domains or subsystems; naming is drifting or overloaded
section_summaries.Inputs: target area or subsystem; current naming and responsibilities
section_summaries.Outputs: named contexts or subsystems; rough boundary map
section_summaries.Procedure: identify the target area and the terms people use for it; separate responsibilities into bounded contexts or subsystems
section_summaries.Contracts: boundaries should reduce semantic confusion, not create ceremony for its own sake; neighboring contexts should be named explicitly when relevant
section_summaries.Risks and anti-patterns: inventing too many contexts for a small problem; renaming concepts without reducing confusion
section_summaries.Verification: confirm the main ambiguity was reduced; confirm interfaces or handoff points are named

section_summaries.Adaptation points: local domain vocabulary; canonical docs that define terminology

interface.default_prompt: Use $aoa-bounded-context-map to name the relevant contexts, clarify what belongs inside each boundary, and note the interfaces between them.

collision_notes: aoa-bounded-context-map is about naming contexts and interfaces at the system or domain level.
aoa-core-logic-boundary is about separating reusable rules from glue or orchestration.
aoa-port-adapter-refactor is about isolating a concrete dependency behind a port or adapter.

explicit: Use $aoa-bounded-context-map to name the relevant contexts, clarify what belongs inside each boundary, and note the interfaces between them.
implicit: Clarify system or domain boundaries, name contexts, and surface interfaces so changes stay semantically scoped. Use when naming is overloaded, responsibilities are mixed, or a task needs a cleaner boundary before coding.
negative: The edit is tiny, fully local, and there is no meaningful ambiguity about boundaries or interfaces.
The terms account, tenant, workspace, and organization are overloaded across this repo. Map the bounded contexts and the interfaces between them before we change code.

Use when two components interact across a meaningful interface, downstream assumptions matter, or a smoke path needs an explicit contract. Do not use for purely local changes or when the real need is invariant or property testing instead of boundary validation.
skills.compatibility: Designed for Codex or similar coding agents with repository file access and an interactive shell. Network access is optional and only needed when repository validation or referenced workflows require it.

section_summaries.Intent: Strengthen boundary reliability by making expected inputs, outputs, and validation summaries explicit.

section_summaries.Inputs: boundary under review; expected inputs and outputs
section_summaries.Outputs: explicit contract assumptions; tests or smoke summary changes
section_summaries.Procedure: identify the boundary and its consumers; state the expected input/output behavior
section_summaries.Contracts: the contract should be visible to another human or agent; verification should be tied to the boundary, not only to internals
section_summaries.Risks and anti-patterns: vague contracts that do not actually constrain behavior; treating a smoke summary as proof when it does not cover the real boundary

skills.name: aoa-core-logic-boundary
skills.description: Separate reusable core logic from glue, orchestration, and infrastructure detail. Use when stable rules are mixed with wiring, the same logic repeats in several places, or reviews are muddy because the responsibility center is unclear. Do not use for tiny isolated fixes or when the real task is introducing a port or adapter around a concrete dependency.
skills.compatibility: Designed for Codex or similar coding agents with repository file access and an interactive shell. Network access is optional and only needed when repository validation or referenced workflows require it.

section_summaries.Intent: Use this skill to separate reusable core logic from surrounding glue, orchestration, or infrastructure detail so future changes land in the right place.
section_summaries.Trigger boundary: a module mixes stable rules with wiring or execution detail; the same logic is starting to appear in several places
section_summaries.Inputs: target module, service, or slice; current responsibilities
section_summaries.Outputs: clarified boundary between core logic and surrounding glue; notes on what should stay reusable versus edge-specific
section_summaries.Procedure: identify which rules or behaviors are stable enough to count as reusable core logic; identify which parts are mostly wiring, orchestration, I/O, or environment detail
section_summaries.Contracts: reusable logic should not stay trapped in glue if that blocks testing or reuse; glue should not be over-promoted into domain logic without reason
section_summaries.Risks and anti-patterns: treating all logic as core logic and over-abstracting the system; moving orchestration detail into the core under the label of purity
section_summaries.Verification: confirm the core candidate is genuinely reusable or stability-shaped; confirm edge-specific code remains at the edge when appropriate

skills.name: aoa-dry-run-first

Use when the action can be previewed and mistakes would be costly, such as delete, restore, migrate, or reconfigure operations. Do not use for purely analytical tasks or when the primary question is whether execution is authorized at all.
skills.compatibility: Designed for Codex or similar coding agents with repository file access and an interactive shell. Network access is optional and only needed when repository validation or referenced workflows require it.

section_summaries.Outputs: dry-run or preview recommendation; bounded preview result or plan
section_summaries.Procedure: identify whether a preview, simulation, inspect-only, or dry-run path exists; prefer the safest bounded preview path before real execution
section_summaries.Contracts: dry-run should reduce uncertainty before execution; preview results should not be overstated
section_summaries.Risks and anti-patterns: treating a preview as complete validation; skipping preview because the real change looks small
section_summaries.Verification: confirm a preview or dry-run path was considered first; confirm the preview scope was named honestly

skills.name: aoa-invariant-coverage-audit
skills.description: Audit whether existing tests and checks actually constrain the stable invariants that matter. Use when you need to judge if current coverage proves a rule or only repeats examples, and you want the smallest bounded follow-up gaps. Do not use when the invariant itself is still undefined or when the real task is to author new invariants rather than audit coverage.
skills.compatibility: Designed for Codex or similar coding agents with repository file access and an interactive shell. Network access is optional and only needed when repository validation or referenced workflows require it.

section_summaries.Intent: Use invariant-oriented coverage to judge whether an existing validation surface really constrains the stable truth, and turn that judgment into a bounded audit package instead of a loose example review.
section_summaries.Trigger boundary: an existing test or check surface needs a review for invariant strength; the question is whether current checks really constrain the stable rule
section_summaries.Inputs: target rule or stable truth; current tests, checks, or examples
section_summaries.Outputs: invariant coverage map; gap list for weak or missing checks
section_summaries.Procedure: name the stable truth in plain language; map each existing check to the invariant it constrains
section_summaries.Contracts: every claimed invariant must be traceable to at least one check; the audit should distinguish real constraint from mere example repetition
section_summaries.Risks and anti-patterns: counting examples as if they were invariants; letting random data hide thin coverage
section_summaries.Verification: confirm each core invariant is named and mapped to a check; confirm the coverage gap list is specific and bounded

aoa-invariant-coverage-audit inspects whether current tests already prove the relevant invariants.

negative: The real problem is that we have not identified the invariant yet, so this is not an audit of existing coverage.
